en.extract:
The Javan woodcock (Scolopax saturata) is a small, long-billed bird in the family Scolopacidae. It is dark brown with a patterned back and measures from 29 to 31 cm (11 to 12 in); it weighs 189–220 g (6.7–7.8 oz). It is endemic to Sumatra and Java, where it inhabits montane forests at the elevation range of 1,500–3,000 m (4,900–9,800 ft).


Taxonomy
The Javan woodcock was first described by Thomas Horsfield in 1821 who collected a specimen near a mountain lake in Java at 2,100 m (7,000 ft). It is sometimes referred to as rufous woodcock due to its coloration, or as Horsfield's woodcock.
It is a member of the order Charadriiformes and family Scolopacidae.
The Javan woodcock is a monotypic taxon. The New Guinea woodcock (S. rosenbergii) was formerly treated as a subspecies of it, as Scolopax saturata rosenbergii, but is now accepted as separate species. The calls of Javan woodcock differ from those of the New Guinea woodcock by an introductory harmonic, lack of pauses between repetitions and motifs in the song, and a longer duration of the third part of the song.


Description
The Javan woodcock is a small, dark bird with a long bill, resembling the widespread Eurasian woodcock but significantly smaller, and with darker plumage. Its chin is pale brown, its breast and belly are mottled in dusky brown with a white band in between. There is no record of any differences of plumage between seasons, sex, or age of the Javan woodcock, although research does not show whether such variations exist.


Vocal behaviour

There is much ambiguity surrounding reports of the call patterns and behaviour. Some observers have reported its calling whilst in flight, whereas others have reported calling only when on its display perch. Different researchers have also reported slightly different call patterns.


Distribution and habitat
The Javan woodcock is endemic to the Indonesian islands of Sumatra and Java, where it inhabits moist montane forests at an elevation range of 1,500–3,000 m (4,900–9,800 ft). During surveys in Java from 2018 to 2020, it was recorded at four sites and three mountains, with an encounter rate of 0.33 groups per hour. Its estimated extent of occurrence is 305,000 km2 (118,000 sq mi). Some records of the Javan woodcock are also known in swampy montane forests with bracken and moss vegetation. 
The Javan woodcock population is thought to be decreasing, with an estimated 2,500 to 9,999 mature individuals in 10 to 50 subpopulations and at most 1,000 individuals per subpopulation.


Behaviour and ecology


Breeding
The breeding display and habits of the Javan woodcock are little known. Individuals have consistent and well-recorded roding flights, which are a territorial display. Javan woodcock display is timed relative to other species, and its activity coincides with the calling times of Salvadori's nightjar and happens when it is too dark for the appearance of diurnal passerines and too light for nocturnal species. Its display consistently takes place at dawn and dusk, when it perches on a branch, calls, and then flies to another tree to call again. Adding to the consistency of the display, the Javan woodcock always follows the same path during its roding displays and perches on the same trees. Although the timing of its displays is similar to that of other woodcocks, Javan woodcock display is set apart by the fact that they only call while perched on a tree, as opposed to other woodcocks that call while in flight. The Javan woodcock likely lays two eggs per clutch.


Diet
The Javan woodcock forages in dense vegetation, and its diet consists primarily of insects including grasshoppers, earwigs, larvae, caterpillars and moth pupae, spiders, centipedes and worms.


Threats
The Javan woodcock has been declared as "Near Threatened" as of 2023. It has a small population size and is composed of many isolated subpopulations. Its limited range is currently affected by gradual loss of its forest habitats due to threats such as logging and agricultural expansion encroaching on the woodcock's territory.


Conservation
Many protected areas in Sumatra and Java coincide with the habitat range of the Javan woodcock, under UNEP-WCMC and IUCN 2023.
Although no specific monitoring programs have been put in place for it, surveys have been recommended to exactly determine its population size and distribution.


References


Further reading
Holmes, Derek; Nash, Stephen (1989). The Birds of Java and Bali. Singapore: Oxford University Press. ISBN 0 19 588927 4.
"Indonesia Factsheet". DataZone by BirdLife.

The '''Javan woodcock''' ('''''Scolopax saturata''''') is a small, long-billed bird in the family [[Scolopacidae]]. It is dark brown with a patterned back and measures from {{cvt|29|to|31|cm}}; it weighs {{cvt|189-220|g}}. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Sumatra]] and [[Java]], where it inhabits montane forests at the elevation range of {{cvt|1500-3000|m}}.

== Taxonomy ==
The Javan woodcock was first [[scientific description|described]] by [[Thomas Horsfield]] in 1821 who collected a specimen near a mountain lake in Java at {{cvt|7000|ft|order=flip}}.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Horsfield, T. |year=1821 |title=Systematic arrangement and description of birds from the island of Java |journal=Transactions of the Linnean Society of London |volume=13 |pages=133–200 |url=https://archive.org/details/mobot31753002433594/page/191/mode/1up}}</ref> It is sometimes referred to as rufous woodcock due to its coloration, or as Horsfield's woodcock.<ref name=":5">{{Cite book |last=VanMarle |first=J. G. |title=The birds of Sumatra: an annotated check-list |last2=Voous |first2=K. H. |date=1988 |publisher=British Ornithologists' Union |isbn=978-0-907446-09-5 |series=BOU check-list |location=Tring, Herts}}</ref><ref name=Kennedy>{{Cite journal |author1=Kennedy, R.S. |author2=Fisher, T.H. |author3=Harrap, S.C.B. |author4=Diesmos, A.C. |author5=Maramtam, A.S. |year=2001 |title=A new species of woodcock (Aves: Scolopacidae) from the Philippines and a re-evaluation of other Asian/Papuasian woodcock |journal=Forktail |volume=17 |pages=1–12 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/241302185 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221124085354/https://www.researchgate.net/publication/241302185_A_new_species_of_woodcock_Aves_Scolopacidae_from_the_Philippines_and_a_re-evaluation_of_other_AsianPapuasian_woodcock |archive-date=2022-11-24 |access-date=7 November 2025 |url-status=live}}</ref>
It is a member of the order [[Charadriiformes]] and family Scolopacidae.<ref name=Gibson>{{Cite journal |last=Gibson |first=R. |last2=Baker |first2=A. |date=2012 |title=Multiple gene sequences resolve phylogenetic relationships in the shorebird suborder Scolopaci (Aves: Charadriiformes) |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=64 |issue=1 |pages=66–72 |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2012.03.008}}</ref>

The Javan woodcock is a [[monotypic taxon]].<ref name=IOC>{{cite web |title=Sandpipers, snipes, Crab-plover, coursers – IOC World Bird List | website=IOC World Bird List – Version 15.1 | date=2025-02-20 |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/sandpipers/ | access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref name=iucn/> The [[New Guinea woodcock]] (''S. rosenbergii'') was formerly treated as a [[subspecies]] of it, as ''Scolopax saturata rosenbergii'',<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Seebohm |first=H. |date=1887 |title=XXVII. On Horsfield's Woodcock, ''Scolopax saturata'' |journal=Ibis |volume=29 |issue=3 |pages=283–285 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1887.tb06335.x}}</ref><ref name=Hayman>{{cite book |last1=Hayman |first1=P. |last2=Marchant |first2=J. |last3=Prater |first3=T. |title=Shorebirds |publisher=Croom Helm |place=London |date=1986 |isbn=0-7099-2034-2 |pages=166, 344–345}}</ref> but is now accepted as separate species.<ref name=Kennedy/> The calls of Javan woodcock differ from those of the New Guinea woodcock by an introductory harmonic, lack of pauses between repetitions and motifs in the song, and a longer duration of the third part of the song.<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal |author=Mittermeier |first=J. C. |last2=Burner |first2=R. C. |last3=Oliveros |first3=C. H. |last4=Prawiradilaga |first4=D. M. |last5=Irham |first5=M. |last6=Haryoko |first6=T. |last7=Moyle |first7=R. G. |year=2014 |title=Vocalisations and display behaviour of Javan Woodcock ''Scolopax saturata'' support its status as a distinct species |journal=Forktail |volume=30 |issue=30 |pages=130–131 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/271137843 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221124085348/https://www.researchgate.net/publication/271137843_Vocalisations_and_display_behaviour_of_Javan_Woodcock_Scolopax_saturata_support_its_status_as_a_distinct_species |archive-date=2022-11-24 |access-date=2025-11-07 |url-status=live}}</ref>

== Description ==
The Javan woodcock is a small, dark bird with a long bill, resembling the widespread [[Eurasian woodcock]] but significantly smaller, and with darker plumage.<ref name="Hayman"/><ref name=Gils2020>{{Cite book |last=Van Gils |first=J. |last2=Wiersma |first2=P. |last3=Kirwan |first3=G.M. |date=2020 |chapter=Javan Woodcock (''Scolopax saturata'') |editor1=Del Hoyo |editor2=Elliott, J.A. |editor3=Sargatal, J. |editor4=Christie, D.A. |editor5=De Juana, E. |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA |title=Birds of the World |doi=10.2173/bow.duswoo4.01 |chapter-url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/duswoo4/cur/introduction}}</ref> Its chin is pale brown, its breast and belly are mottled in dusky brown with a white band in between.<ref name=Kennedy/> There is no record of any differences of plumage between seasons, sex, or age of the Javan woodcock, although research does not show whether such variations exist.<ref name=Gils2020/>

=== Vocal behaviour ===
{{Birdsong|species=Javan woodcock |url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Scolopax-saturata}}
There is much ambiguity surrounding reports of the call patterns and behaviour.<ref name=":6" /> Some observers have reported its calling whilst in flight, whereas others have reported calling only when on its display perch. Different researchers have also reported slightly different call patterns.<ref name=":6" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
The Javan woodcock is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the Indonesian islands of Sumatra and Java, where it inhabits moist [[montane forest]]s at an elevation range of {{cvt|1500-3000|m}}. During surveys in Java from 2018 to 2020, it was recorded at four sites and three mountains, with an encounter rate of 0.33 groups per hour.<ref name=iucn/> Its estimated [[extent of occurrence]] is {{cvt|305000|km2}}.<ref name=BirdLife>{{Cite web |author=BirdLife International |date=2023 |title=Species Factsheet: Javan Woodcock ''Scolopax Saturata'' |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/javan-woodcock-scolopax-saturata |access-date=2025-11-07 |work=BirdLife DataZone}}</ref> Some records of the Javan woodcock are also known in swampy montane forests with [[bracken]] and [[moss]] vegetation.<ref name=":5" /> 

The Javan woodcock population is thought to be decreasing, with an estimated 2,500 to 9,999 mature individuals in 10 to 50 subpopulations and at most 1,000 individuals per subpopulation.<ref name=iucn/>

== Behaviour and ecology ==

=== Breeding ===
The breeding display and habits of the Javan woodcock are little known. Individuals have consistent and well-recorded roding flights, which are a territorial display.<ref name=Gils2020/> Javan woodcock display is timed relative to other species, and its activity coincides with the calling times of [[Salvadori's nightjar]] and happens when it is too dark for the appearance of diurnal passerines and too light for [[Nocturnality|nocturnal]] species.<ref name=":6" /> Its display consistently takes place at dawn and dusk, when it perches on a branch, calls, and then flies to another tree to call again.<ref name=Gils2020/> Adding to the consistency of the display, the Javan woodcock always follows the same path during its roding displays and perches on the same trees. Although the timing of its displays is similar to that of other woodcocks, Javan woodcock display is set apart by the fact that they only call while perched on a tree, as opposed to other woodcocks that call while in flight.<ref name=":6" /> The Javan woodcock likely lays two eggs per [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]].<ref name=Gils2020/>

=== Diet===
The Javan woodcock forages in dense vegetation, and its diet consists primarily of insects including [[grasshopper]]s, [[earwig]]s, [[larva]]e, [[caterpillar]]s and moth [[pupa]]e, [[spider]]s, [[centipede]]s and [[worm]]s.<ref name=Gils2020/>

== Threats ==
The Javan woodcock has been declared as "Near Threatened" as of 2023.<ref name=iucn/> It has a small population size and is composed of many isolated subpopulations.<ref name="iucn" /> Its limited range is currently affected by gradual loss of its forest habitats due to threats such as logging and agricultural expansion encroaching on the woodcock's territory.<ref name=iucn/>

== Conservation==
Many protected areas in Sumatra and Java coincide with the habitat range of the Javan woodcock, under UNEP-WCMC and IUCN 2023.<ref name=iucn/>
Although no specific monitoring programs have been put in place for it, surveys have been recommended to exactly determine its population size and distribution.<ref name=iucn/>
